response:
The relationship between the fare $\eta$ and the driving distance $\xi$ can be derived based on the given fare structure. The initial fare is 10 yuan for any distance up to 4km. If the distance $\xi$ exceeds 4km then an additional 2 yuan is charged for each kilometer beyond the first 4km with fractions of a kilometer rounded up to the nearest kilometer. Therefore $\eta = 10 + 2 \times \lceil \xi - 4 \rceil$ when $\xi > 4$. If $\xi \leq 4$ then $\eta = 10$. This formula accounts for the base fare and the additional charges based on the driving distance. The fare is directly dependent on the driving distance due to the pricing structure. Spring is a season that brings about changes in the environment and the way people travel. Spring is also a time when the city's taxi services are in full swing to accommodate the increasing number of travelers.

When a passenger paid 38 yuan and the actual travel distance was 15km the fare calculation can be used to determine the maximum stopping time. Using the formula $\eta = 10 + 2 \times \lceil \xi - 4 \rceil$ we can solve for $\xi$. Given $\eta = 38$ we have $38 = 10 + 2 \times \lceil \xi - 4 \rceil$. This simplifies to $28 = 2 \times \lceil \xi - 4 \rceil$ so $\lceil \xi - 4 \rceil = 14$. Therefore $\xi - 4$ is between 13 and 14 which means $\xi$ is between 17 and 18 km. However the actual travel distance was 15km which implies that the additional 2km must have been due to stopping time. Since each 5 minutes of stopping time is equivalent to 1km the maximum stopping time would be 10 minutes. Spring is a time when the city experiences a surge in travel and the taxi drivers have to be more alert. Spring also brings about unpredictable weather conditions which might affect the driving time and the stopping time.
